title: Building a small pond for surface water management
subtitle: A flexible-liner wildlife pond that also slows and infiltrates roof and driveway runoff
excerpt: A 2 to 4 m² flexible-liner pond at the lowest point of a garden can absorb roof water from one downpipe, reduce fast runoff from impermeable surfaces, and create a wildlife habitat. This is the site selection, excavation, liner installation, and overflow design for a combined wildlife and surface-water management pond.
sourceNotes: CIRIA SuDS Manual (C753, 2015) on ponds and swales for surface water management. Royal Horticultural Society 'Wildlife pond' guidance. The Wildlife Trusts pond creation factsheets. DEFRA 'Sustainable drainage: non-statutory technical standards' (2015).

glossaryTerms.definition: A butyl rubber or EPDM sheet laid into an excavated pond shape to retain water. Butyl rubber is more durable and puncture-resistant; EPDM is more flexible and suitable for complex shapes.

A defined path for water to leave a pond when it reaches maximum level during a storm event. Without an overflow route, water spreads uncontrolled. A notched edge, overflow pipe, or a downstream swale or soakaway constitutes an overflow route.

Systems (SuDS) that manage surface water by mimicking natural processes: infiltration, storage, and gradual release. Ponds, swales, and permeable paving are all SuDS components. They reduce the peak rate of water entering drains during storm events.

projectSchedule.title: Dig and line the pond
projectSchedule.body: Excavate, install the liner, and connect the inflow. Fill with water initially from tap or water butt.

projectSchedule.title: Add aquatic plants
projectSchedule.body: Once the water has settled and cleared, plant marginals and submerged oxygenators.

projectSchedule.title: First season check
projectSchedule.body: Check water level, overflow route, and liner edges for erosion. Note any wildlife activity.

content.text: A small garden pond creates habitat for frogs, newts, insects, and birds, and stores heavy rainfall that would otherwise run off into the drain. A 2 m² pond 600 mm deep holds 1.2 m³ of water, enough to absorb runoff from around 25 to 30 m² of roof in a moderate rain event. With an
overflow route
to a soakaway or lawn, it becomes part of a
sustainable drainage
network.
Site selection
Choose the lowest point in the garden, downhill from the downpipe or driveway being managed
Minimum 3 m from any building foundation (pond water saturates the surrounding soil)
Avoid locations directly under deciduous trees: leaf fall adds nutrients and promotes algae
Partial shade is fine. Full sun promotes evaporation and algae. Full shade reduces wildlife value.
Excavation and liner sizing
Mark the pond outline on the ground. A kidney or irregular shape fits better into a garden edge than a rectangle and creates more marginal planting opportunities.

Excavate in zones: a shallow marginal shelf 200 mm deep around part of the perimeter, a mid-zone at 400 mm, and a deep zone at 600 to 700 mm. The graduated depth reduces algae by creating different temperature zones.

Calculate liner size: length = maximum pond length plus twice the maximum depth plus 500 mm overhang. Same calculation for width. For a 2.5 x 2 m pond, 700 mm deep: liner = (2.5 + 1.4 + 0.5) x (2 + 1.4 + 0.5) = 4.4 x 3.9 m.
Install 50 mm of sharp sand or fibrous underlay (sold as pond liner underlay) over the entire excavated surface before the liner. Remove any protruding stones.
Drape the
flexible liner
over the excavation. Add water weight gradually to pull the liner into shape; fold pleats at the corners as neatly as you can rather than fighting the wrinkles.
Trim the liner leaving 300 mm overlap at the edge. Peg the edge down with large flat stones or turf.
Inflow and overflow

Route a downpipe via a first-flush diverter into the pond at the shallow marginal end. Place a stone diffuser under the inlet to slow the incoming flow and prevent scouring the liner. Route the overflow notch or standpipe to a lawn area, a soakaway, or a secondary water butt 150 mm lower in the garden. The overflow must be lower than the liner edge to prevent the liner lifting during a storm.

attrs.symptom: Pond level drops by 25 mm per week in summer

content.text: This is normal evaporation in warm weather: a 2 m² pond loses 50 litres per week at peak summer. Top up with water butt water, not tap water, to avoid chlorine affecting aquatic life.

attrs.symptom: Green water algae within weeks of filling

content.text: Normal in a new pond. Add submerged oxygenating plants (hornwort, water crowfoot) which outcompete algae for nutrients. Avoid fertilisers near the pond. Green water usually clears within 4 to 6 weeks once plants establish.
